Fantastic. So as I mentioned, this is week two. This is Tell the Story. And we have a wonderful program. We've got a lot of very interesting businesses and deals that our students have submitted to us and I worked with them yesterday to kind of download the raw materials of their pitch. And Orrin has seen that. He's prepped. Ready to go. So I want to thank everyone for joining us today. And without further ado, Orrin. Okay, great. Yeah, great. Thanks, David. All right. Hey, David, how many people approximately do we have on right now? Attending right now, we have 30 people. Okay, great. Just get a sense of it. All right, guys. Hey, yeah. Like I said, I'm in New York. It's going to be interesting. And I mentioned this because it's a validating point. I may have mentioned it before, but I'll repeat it. One of the very largest ad agencies in the world has flown me out here to help them on pitching. So normally I don't talk about my investment banking or clients in such detail. But when an ad agency that is pitching to Copper Tone and GM and Visa and calls you in to say, hey, we love this style of pitching. We don't know how to do it. I mean, it's very validating for the material. I mean, I'm not sure I need any more validation because we go out and do this every day. And some of the students that we've been working with have changed. We've had some successes. People have already raised money. But when an ad agency says help us pitch, that's very exciting. So it's cool to be out here in New York. So this module is very close to my heart. I mean, this is tell the story. This is the pitch. This is where you communicate where you actually have, what you actually have. If you don't nail it, then you can't kind of do all the other stuff because people don't know what you're selling, what your deal is, what it's all about, what the value proposition is. So we wanted to get in as many, rather than talking theory, as many actual case studies and real world problems as possible on this call. Before we go to the first one, I just want to give a quick summary in my mind what I think is achievable here. So first of all, we've got to help people, ourselves, and our friends on this call and the people we work with communicate the big idea. What the hell is going on here? Why are we involved with this? A lot of this, I mean, you can think of meeting your buddy for the first time in five years, right? And he says, John, Dave, Orin, Megan, Susan, whatever. What have you been up to? What are you doing? Right? That's really the essence of the big idea. You're explaining them what you're working on, why it's important, how it's changing the world or changing the world you live in. What's big about it? Why is it important? What's the big idea? And how's the big idea relevant to what's happening today? What's changing in your market that you're taking advantage of? Sometimes we forget. Business is not taking advantage. So what's the big idea and what are we taking advantage of? And the next thing is, what's the problem? Why did we get involved with this in the first place? What did we recognize that is difficult to do? And once we explain what the problem is that we have a solution for, we admit that there's other people that can solve this problem. They may not solve it as well as us. They may not be as good as us. They may not be as committed or passionate as us, but other people can solve this problem. I mean, McDonald's serves an incredible hamburger. You go to McDonald's in Australia or South Africa or Manhattan, although those are countries in Manhattan City. But anyway, you go to different McDonald's in different places, they serve a uniform product. But there's lots of other places that serve hamburger. I mean, we're not the only person we admit that other people can provide a solution. And then in context of all that, we provide our solution. Provide our solution. Then we're going to talk about how it works and really communicate a clear value proposition. I think that's what it's the most we could do. But if you do just that, the big idea, what's changing? Next the problem is how people solve this problem generically, how our solution is better than the generic solutions, how it works in the value proposition. And we make that real. Then I think we've told at least to some degree a good story, and then we can pile on the other telling the story stuff. But I want to make sure that people present today at least have those components nailed. David, does that make sense to you? Okay, I'm just going to assume you said yes. Sorry, I needed my mic. As you were talking, that sounds great. I think that those components do align with the shared work. We've got a lot of people who gave us... Hey, David, your mic is on underwater mode, underwater dark side of the moon. So I think you just switch over to the first case study, then let's just get started on it. Is this any clear? Nope, it is not. Computer, how is that? A little bit better. I think if we get started with the first guy, then we or Gal, then we'll be rolling. Okay, great. Hey, Joe, welcome to the call. Hey, how are you, David? How's it going, Orin? Great, great, great. Doing great. Jumping on. So to make this valuable for people listening, let's start cranking. All right. So Joe, you want to kind of go and give us your pitch as you've structured it. And let me see if it's perfect. I'll stay quiet. But if there's points I can jump in to strengthen it and illustrate some of these points, I will definitely speak up and do it. Okay, to set the landscape real quick, typically we're in front of anywhere from 10 to 20 people on the larger deals. And it is government. We sell the dispatch centers, Orin, to just give you an idea of what is a government sale. Anyway, so what we do is we focus on next generation 911 and dispatch improvement, and we start out by asking a question, what's the biggest revolution going on right now in 911? And typically the people will answer and get the group involved. And then it's the impact of next generation 911. By the way, that's a real term. For those in the audience that don't know, most people think that you can simply text 911 and they get it. So if you're hiding in a closet with some prowler in your house, they do not get it. And so it's a real problem. So that's the change agent that's going on in the market right now. We focus on the recording side there. We're experts in that arena. We focus on dispatch improvement because the bottom line is second save lives. So that's where we live and what we do. And again, there's also a revolution going on in the PSAP recording market, the public safety ants, the police recording market. Now, out of role play for a sec, we sell recorders. So that's what we do. So if you dial 911, the call is recorded for the group just to give you an idea of what I'm talking about. In any buzzword I happen to use in the pitch, the audience knows what it means. So we're not alienating people with buzzwords or language and that sort of thing. Sure. OK. So back into the pitch. Go ahead. Yeah, go ahead. Take it off and we'll be your audience. You pitch us and if something goes, if the tree goes off the tracks, I will jump in. OK, all right. Good. So anyway, there's a revolution happening right now in the recording market. And that's what we're going to show you today, our Equiture Next Generation 911 recording platform. Now, for those of you sitting in the audience, why is this important to you? I'd be asking the same thing. What's the big deal? We're going to focus on three core areas of simplicity, reliability, and speed, because it all comes down to saving time. Then we outline why we're the best and we focus on three core components, our direct advantage business model, revolutionary search, and Next Gen 911. Now, the big problem is that the... We understand by asking and talking to the audience before our presentation today that Next Generation is coming and there's a lot of change there. And we... Our chief technologist sits on those boards and so we're very... We are leading the area in regards to Next Generation 911. So we'll highlight our expertise and show you exactly what's happening and what is changing now. So Joe, and I see this a lot... So Lauren, on a role play for a second, that's kind of how we frame it. Yeah. So I wouldn't worry about framing it. People are a lot smarter than you think. I would just go for the pitch. Okay. I see a lot of people. You don't give macro explanations. Just dive into it. That's going to be what's most valuable. And then for people listening, I want you to take note. I do this myself. I try and like... When I go to practice a pitch, I'll give macro level notes. Well, now I'd be standing up and sitting in the room. And well, now my audience already knows about this. You know, I say many things, but when you're practicing your pitch, don't narrate what's happening. I think it's going to be most valuable to you if you just go in and pump it out. We'll get it. And if we don't know a term, right, and you're doing your job, we will... One term doesn't make or break a pitch. It will get what's going on from the essence of it. So just go for it. Give the pitch. Okay. All right. So back with the simplicity, reliability, and speed. You know, John, you had mentioned that you're having nothing but problems with your current system. It's very unreliable. It's not working. It takes forever to find stuff. We'll address that later as we dive in. Why is Equiture the best? We focus on three things. DSS Direct Advantage, Revolutionary Search, and our next generation 911 expertise, which we will highlight here. Now, if I'm sitting where you're at, I'm thinking to myself, yeah, right, prove it. So we prove it two ways. Number one, with our clients, and then I play a video testimonial so they see it. And then also, we put our money where our mouth is with 100% money back guaranteed. Now, on the first portion is the DSS Direct Advantage, where we're unique in the market is we make the platform, we sell it, we install it, we train it. Okay, so that eliminates finger pointing. Now, you guys can't see the picture, but I got a picture of George Bush and Obama pointing at each other, kind of, you know, so light in the room. Our Revolutionary Search, you know, it's interesting. Everybody in this market besides us can obviously record, but we put them in what's called pre-Google. Can anybody remember looking for information before Google? Very difficult. And basically, it's like doing a term paper in college back using encyclopedias and typewriters. Well, the reason everybody else besides us is pre-Google is because you can only search on 5% of the information, like call, phone number, the person that took the call, and this sort of thing. Equature is the only post-Google platform because we can search on 100% of the content. So that means that if a caller calls up and, you know, gunshots fired, you can search for gunshots fired and we'll find the recording based on what the caller said. The last portion is our next generation 911 functionality. And we focus there. Guys, then we go into a 15-minute briefing, which is not going to be relevant for the group here. Now, are we the biggest? And the answer there is no. We're not. Our market sharing recorders parallels that of Apple in the computer business. And like Apple, we strive to make simple, reliable, and great solutions for our customers. And that's basically it in a nutshell. Okay. All right. Good. Thanks, Joe. All right. So let me see here. I want to comment. Then I want to go back through the structure. I want to try and force you to adapt. Right? So the big idea to me, and I don't know your business well enough, but I'm thinking about it, right? So 911 recording is changing. The technology, obviously recording technologies and search technologies, okay, and redundancy technologies have improved significantly year over year for the last 10 years. But now they're at an inflection point where they're not only improved, but they're reliable and cost efficient. And this is why we're now able to introduce a new technology. We're now able to introduce two new products. So the big idea is going post-limited search. The big idea is 100% search penetration. All right? So our new product suite, XYZ, is the first cost-efficient 911 high functionality, 100% search implementation, right, with a 30-day implementation timeline and a local government affordability. That's the big idea. So look, here's the problem today. A call comes in, right? You get a mention of gunshots fired. That gets recorded and then out at the, you know, the parameters where agents or officers are looking for information, they can search on it and find that call. It's a real problem today. Let me give you two examples that are own customers have experienced, right? Example one, a woman died when a search failed. Example two, a business owner lost a warehouse full of $5 million of material that our search or anybody with 100% search would have succeeded with. All right? It's a real problem. Now look, today, people are solving this. Obviously, crimes get solved and prevented all the time. But what it does is it takes very, very experienced operators, okay, who have 10 or 20 years of experience who know how to operate these Clujie systems, right, or just are able to know how these networks work, all right? So it can be done today. But not, our solution is 100% digital and it's consensable to the whole network and cost-effective. And here are some details on our solution. And let me tell you how it works. It's kind of interesting, right? We have a licensed algorithm that is a search algorithm and our chief technology officer has integrated this with equipment. There's over five years of research and development integrating these technologies. And I'll give you some specifics on how it works. It's very interesting, you know, the way you need software, monomics, and hardware to all work together. I'll tell you about some of our integration. So ultimately, I want to be very clear about our value proposition. For local departments, all right, that are still working on 5, 10, 15, 20-year-old systems, including them together, where the costs are high, the downtime is significant, and most importantly, there's a frustration because of the inability to search incidents in real time. Our solution is a 100% search fully integrated local government, IEE AAA compliant implementation that falls within departmental budgets. Okay. So, Joe, well, I don't know your product well enough. Do you see the structure that I was following? And does it feel any different to you? And as we look at it together, is there any contrast between the two presentations? Sure. No, I mean, actually, couple things. Number one, the five years of R&D is through his reel. And Scott, welcome. Hello? Hey there. Hi, how are you? Okay, you're on the call. Orrin, take it away. Yeah. All right Scott, so what I want you to do is not narrate where you are within the pitch and if something in your business is not explainable to us, don't tell us. Don't jump out of your pitch, just dive in and give it. We're a live audience. Yeah, sure. Okay, go for it. Great. Okay, so where are you and me? Which means by nature we enjoy physical interactions. We're storytellers. And greeting cards is the modern day cave painting. Only now this cave man can put that image in front of anyone who has a physical mailing address. And today billions of cards are being sent every year, mostly on holidays and lots of other occasions as well. But let's face it, greeting cards has lost their attraction a long time ago. And that's because there are two major problems with greeting cards today. You know what they are. Firstly, with today's modern technology everybody's going online. And this means our interactions is getting more and more impersonal with Facebook and Twitter and everything. And that's just how it is. And secondly, the designs of the greeting cards are boring, humorless, tacky and tasteless. Which means that the only one who likes them is grandma or someone who is 75 years old. So sending greeting cards is pain, time consuming and the design plain out sucks. So but there are solutions today in this market. I'm going to jump in because I promise if the train was going off the tracks that I would jump in. And this is in an unexpected way. But this is a very common problem that we've never talked about. Your microphone sucks. So the reason that I'm bringing this up is I hear lots of times guys call in to pitch me or the funds that I work with at deal. You can't friggin hear what they are saying. So I'm going to call you out on this. You've got to get your technology worked out. And I'll tell you, I had the same problem. But I call guys and say, hey, can you hear me? I went out and bought a $500 headset that truckers use. It's got these noise filters that can filter out the noise of a truck going down a freeway and still have conversation with their dispatcher. So I know you're excited to give this one, but your technology is interfering with your cognitive structure of your pitch because it's so difficult to hear. So we're going to invite you on next time. Right. Because I like where you're going, but we've got to get you a better technology. And I think it's very instructive. If you don't know how this stuff is being received on the other side, you know, with some kind of technology check, then it can be an interference to your pitch even if you've got all the structure 100% perfect and the delivery perfect. And I want to say one other thing that I've found. So Skype. You know, I have to do a lot of calls on Skype. You can't hear you on Skype and you change your headset. Right. You actually have to exit Skype and reenter it for Skype to acknowledge that change. I've blown up a bunch of presentations myself without knowing that. All right. So I want to invite you on next time, but you've got to find somebody to do a technology check with. All right. So we can hear you more clearly. I'll do it. Okay, buddy. All right. Thanks. We'll talk to you on the next call. All right. Have a good time. Thanks, Scott. Okay. Let's move on to the next one. The next one is very interesting deal from Anthony over at Soul Safari. Anthony, will you raise your hand and then we'll unmute you and bring you on the call? There he is. Okay. And welcome to the call. Anthony. Good day, David. How are you, Mike? I'm doing well. You're here with the Oren. Oren, take it away. Good day, Oren. How are you, Mike? I'm doing well, thank you for asking. Doing well. And I'm excited to hear about, you know, we met at the conference, so it was good to be able to follow up. And I'm excited to hear about this one. Yeah, fabulous. Guys, look, I've only got about seven minutes to give you the big idea, so let's jump into it right away so we don't waste anybody's time. All right. As I noticed when I was working in one of the largest services firms in the world in Accenture as the National Practice Director and when I was working in British aerospace, when I was working in the Military Academy, West Point Military Academy, and also now when I'm on the global board of a number of charities, is it's really bloody hard to get people to play nicely. Have you ever noticed when you get a group of people together that you get into a place where there's just those people who don't like working with other people? And you've got an agenda, you've got your mission statement, you've got your plan, you've got your corporate objectives, but these people aren't wanting to play together. And one of the things we've noticed really clearly is that profiling people for your workforce is a big, big issue. And the thing that we always say is hire slowly, fire fast because it costs so much to hire people. It costs so much to dismiss somebody nowadays with the unfair dismissal laws that we have. People are finding that there's problems with letting someone go. There might be accusations of sexual harassment or all sorts of things or because of the color of their skin or their ethnicity. Well, this problem is affecting corporates, it's affecting non-for-profit charities, it's affecting all sorts of people throughout our community and our business life and they're looking for solutions. And the solutions that people have come up with in the past have been to put together HR departments who've got a recruiting formula. So one of the ones at Booz Allen that we were consulting for had a peer approval system. You had to go through and convince everybody in the firm that you were a good guy before you could be brought in as a director. Another way that's been used in Accenture was we would do psychological studies. So you've got to get the person there, you've got to get a shrink to sit down with this person and walk them through hours and hours and hours of profiling. Or you might have heard of things like the Myers-Briggs studies, the disk profiling, the Berkman studies that have been doing personality profiles and then there's the classic that we've all tried. When you go to hire somebody it's called trial and error. Let's get them in, have a go, see how they go and if it's no good let's get rid of them. The cost to corporations is enormous. What we've found in the non-for-profit arena is that we suffer greatly getting people who don't want to work because the people who come to us are volunteers. And so it's even more critical for us as we go back five steps. We go forward one and go back five because we couldn't figure out what people were like. The solution that we've come up with is a really interesting one and it happens to match what we call the triune brain. And there's a new mentor of mine that I've just recently met who talks about the three-part brain and he likes to call the reptile brain the croc brain, you know the crocodile brain. Then there's the social brain and then there's the human brain. And the crocodile brain is that fear, that fight or that freeze. So you've got fight, flight or freeze and that reactive, instinctive part of our brain that tells us whether we're going to get eaten by a lion or not. That is something that personality profiling has never been able to accomplish in the past. Everybody else has been looking at personality. The personality, the problem with that is that under stress personality can change. So you've got somebody in the office, you've got somebody in your workplace, I'm sure you can relate to, where they get stressed and they start to turn into this Dr. Jack or Mr. Hyde type of person where they, completely different. You go, oh my God, I never knew that she was like that. Or you've got a situation where there's maybe, you know, the situation is demanding something different of them. They're required to step into a role that they're not familiar with. So they'll use all sorts of compensating mechanisms to adjust themselves to the new context. But how do you know how they're really, truly going to act with someone else? So what we did is we got an opportunity that was a really rare opportunity. There was a fellow called Dick Nelson who was an engineer and a psychologist who realized that he could get to the- Anthony, I'm going to jump in here. You're doing fine. Okay. You're doing fine. But I think we've got a bunch of this, okay? We've got a bunch of this pitched so far and there's some things we can do to tighten up. Okay. So I want to work on what's happened so far rather than get further in. There's nothing, I'm not interrupting because the train is off the tracks, but I want to help you tighten up what we've heard so far. And I think it's also going to be instructive for people listening. Okay? Sweet. Go for it. All right. So first of all, what I feel happening here is it's very conceptual and you need to tie some proof points underneath these concepts. All right? So when you- and I'll give you a perfect example. You said the cost is enormous. You cannot do that. All right? That is a Pandora's box because the whole time I'm wondering, well, what's enormous? Because enormous to you might be $25. And enormous to me is $25 million. All right? So here's the rule. Here's the rule. You cannot introduce terms that you haven't- that are not defined. All right? So enormous, you know, unless it's a kind of a light, you know, humorous point you're making, but when you say the cost is enormous, right, we have to quantify that. And then that's so- that is one thing I want you to think about. This is all good stuff, but it's conceptual. So it needs to be underwritten by proof points and not, you know, long diatribes of proof points, not that you would, okay? And when you say the cost is enormous, say for example, in the- and we need to hone it into the market that you're specifically talking about. All right? This is very broad in scope. It is- Good. It's our estimate in projections and also based on what we've seen in the New York Times and also in the Wall Street Journal. The cost to nonprofits of poor hiring and their volunteer staff, right, in the top 50 nonprofits is a billion five a year, or is it 400 million a year? It's 250 million a year, right? Let me bring that into perspective from a cost basis for a 200 person nonprofit. The cost of weak hiring practices can be the difference between losing and making money, right? So we want to get some proof points underneath the concepts. So then secondly, I think you went right into the problem without opening up the big idea, okay? And so let me just give you a big idea for you to think about. This won't be the one you end up with, but- because I don't know your business well enough, right? Here's the big idea. Nonprofits are incredibly important to our society. There's three that you may not know about, right? But if they didn't exist, pain and suffering would be more prevalent in the culture we're in, right? One is human trafficking. Two is in animal welfare. And three is for a disease you may not be familiar with, Tasex, but it dramatically affects thousands of Americans and many thousands of people across the world. And so these are examples of nonprofits, okay? If you could make nonprofits more efficient, there would be more of them and it would be more good in the world. And by making them more efficient of all the things you could do, the number one is to hire better. This presentation is about a way that you could significantly impact the quality of hiring in a nonprofit and literally change the amount of good in the world. I'm going to discuss some specifics about that right now. Let me tell you about the problem. And I've experienced this myself, right? And you can kind of deepen the problem where you were. And as you're deepening the problem, underwrite the problem with these proof points. So I think start with the big idea and then go into the problem with proof points and that will be significantly tightened up. I didn't want to cut you short, but I also didn't want you to move on without fixing or strengthening those two areas. I love that. Is this something you're willing to work on? Absolutely. Okay. All right. And then we'll bring you back around because the other thing I want to say is you have some real advantages. You've got great technology. You've got a very clear speaking voice. And I've found myself being mesmerized a little bit. I wanted to hear the whole story. Like I wanted to jump in on the big idea, quite frankly, right? But I wanted to hear the story as well. So I really think you're a good pitch guy. You've got good timbre and cadence to your voice. And there was something slightly hypnotic about it. I wanted to hear the rest of the story, but then I had to jump in because I want you to get the big idea straight. Absolutely. Thank you. Okay. All right, buddy. Cheese. Okay. Yeah, Anthony. I'll talk to you again soon. Thanks, Anthony. All right. Next up, we have Richard Zreich. Richard, can you raise your hand and we'll bring you on the call? Thanks, Richard. Okay. Oran, is there anything that you want to say before we bring Richard on? Yeah. I want to make one other point for Anthony, even though he's off the call now before we get started with Richard. The other thing that I needed to have, just looking at my notes, because he's in non-profit and he's volunteers, I wanted human stories. I wanted examples of human beings experiencing these impacts. And because he doesn't make semiconductors or like, but Joe before him and Anthony, I mean, these guys have two amazing businesses which are ripe with opportunity to talk about human dramas. And human dramas feel accurate because we can relate to them and things that we can relate to and feel accurate, feel truthful and lend a layer of truth to everything we're saying. So both those guys have amazing opportunities to really get specific about human dramas. All right. I wanted Anthony to have that as well. So the big idea for him, get underwriting the universal cost of enormous with proof points. And the third thing for Anthony is really quickly, but digging into some actual human dramas that are related to the field. And we'll lend a social layer of truth to it. Not that it's not truthful, but it will feel to people in the gut when you involve human stories and dramas, it will feel much more accessible. So, okay. Now we can move on. Okay, Orin, a really quick question right before we get into Richard's pitch. Can you recommend the headset that you use so that we can... The other thing is, Scott, I would recommend being wired, especially since you're overseas, not Wi-Fi. But what's a good headset that you recommend, Orin? A couple people are asking us. So on my website, I sell a headset. No. It's off topic. I recognize that. Everybody gets mad at us because you don't sell anything. All right. Hold on. Let me take this off and look at it. This is the Blue Parrot. We 250 XT plus. Blue Parrot. 250 XT plus. Blue Parrot. Listen, now that we're on the subject, it comes with this friggin' pad. I'm going to have the pad mounted to it. The pad accelerates the sound right into your ear drum and it's too loud. Take the pad that comes with it off and put on the foam that comes with it. I doubled up the foam. Otherwise, it's too loud. That's the fix because it's for truckers. It's super, super loud. If you're in your office, it blows your ear drums out. Anyway, that's the one I recommend. Awesome. Thank you. Richard, I hope you're ready. We're going to bring it on to the call now. Hey, Richard. Welcome. Hi. Richard. Hi, Orin. Hello, everyone. I'd like to take the next three minutes to pitch to you, Ikesh Brand. Brands are having a tough time with their marketing ROI and connecting with their consumers online and offline. In the online world where we have better measurement, CDRs or the click-through rates are averaging 0.1 percent and within Facebook it is 0.05 percent and while in the offline world, returns are not better either. So, you're looking at a fraction of 1 percent of the people that you reach out to are actually connecting with you. This translates into billions of dollars being left on the table while consumers are connecting with brands less and less. By our estimate and what we have seen in the marketing studies, it is around $35 billion a year. Our big idea, ladies and gentlemen, is to make brands more profitable by minimizing the leaks and by going straight to that consumer with the money, giving them a stake in the process. My name is Richard Drake and in the next two minutes I'd like to tell you about Ikesh Brand. I've been managing big marketing budgets for the past 17 years with multinationals like Unilever, Keystone Yarlway, Carrier and finally Nissan where I've seen how a lot of money was left on the table while consumers were connecting with us less and less. I started to obsess over a solution over four years ago and when I went to the VP of marketing for Nissan three years ago and I said, I have the solution, let's invest our marketing dollar directly in the consumer and give her a stake in the process. And I got this deer in headlights look. It was humiliating. I went home that day with more determination and I kept at it for three years with several product iterations and a couple of pivots with $130,000 of my own savings and now we have a breakthrough product going live next week. I'm very passionate about companies that grow profitable with the consumer and not despite the consumer and giving back in meaningful ways to society is going to be the biggest achievement of my life. We're in a good position today and we acknowledge that companies like Community Space and Best Agents have been catering to brands and supporting them to understand and how to navigate the social media world. No one is approaching it from a meaningful angle like we are. We're setting out to make meaning and give the consumer a stake in the marketing process while connecting her with the brand on a one-to-one basis. So for Jane, a working mom of two children who now has no say and no stake in the brand's marketing, Icash Brand brings her the power to connect and earn an average of $2,000 a month while giving the brands she loves the ability to be more effective, make meaning while connecting with Jane one-to-one. We start by routing 70% of the advertising money that comes from Jane's brand directly to her and build on her revenue model as Jane starts to influence her brand's product design, retail environment, research and a whole gamma of other areas she could influence by socializing with these brands through our platform. The window of opportunity is wide open for us to create a billion-dollar company in two to three years and give you a 50X return on your $5 million investment for the following three reasons. Brands are shifting massive marketing budgets from offline to online. Millions like Jane around the world are looking for creative solutions to get them out of their economic gold drills and social media advertising revenue is forecast to reach $9.8 billion in 2016 up from an estimated $3.8 billion in 2011, a compounded annual growth rate of 21% ladies and gentlemen. This is how we're going to create a revolution. Thank you. All right. Excellent. So there's some very good things about here. I could really about this. We need to tighten it up. It's excellent because you did it clearly speaking. What I love is you gave a time constraint and the danger in that is to say, I'm going to take up two minutes or 20 minutes of your time and then not doing it because it makes you not credible. But to say I'm going to do it in two minutes and do it in two minutes is awesome. It makes you a person who says something, can follow through on it and is truthful. I love that. Hello? I can hear you. Great. Can you hear me? Yes, welcome, Clint. All right, awesome. I know I don't have a lot of time. I'm going to try to make this happen in five minutes. This pitch is about entrepreneurs, it's about authors, and it's about executives. And I myself am an entrepreneur and an author. And the big problem that we have in our lives and in our careers is the problem of differentiating ourselves from everybody else. There's a million authors, there's a million entrepreneurs, and there's a million executives in every corporation. And over the last 18 months, I have discovered a huge opportunity for entrepreneurs, authors, speakers, and even executives to really distinguish themselves from all their competition using mass media, television news, interviews, and talk shows. And what happened to me was that back in 1994, I published a book with Penguin USA, largest publisher in the world. And when they were promoting the book, it was awesome. My co-author was on all the big TV shows, and we sold 80,000 books. But as their interest went away and they started to no longer promote the book, we were left impotent. We had no way to get the message out there anymore. Well times have changed, and publishing has changed. And what has happened is that there are now a whole bunch of PR firms out there that will book you on television if you pay them high fees, like $3,000 a month. Or even in some cases on a case-by-case basis, if they put you on a TV show, it's $2,500 to get you on Good Morning America, or $1,500 to get you on a local TV news interview. So I paid $6,000 about two years ago, and booked myself onto four local TV news interviews through one of those pay-for-placement services. And quite frankly, I wasn't very good when I made my appearances on ABC and Phoenix, and Fox and Sacramento, and ABC and Sacramento, and another station in Salt Lake City. I spent $6,000 and had basically four pieces of worthless videotape for my results. And what I knew that had to happen was that I had to learn how to book myself on TV news programs so that I could get better at it. So that one day, when Good Morning America discovered me and wanted to put me on, I would have some great TV news footage to show them that I could be a great guest. Well, it took me months, but I finally broke the code. I finally figured out how to book myself on TV news programs. And I got really good at it. In my first year, I booked myself on 18 shows. It's been two years that I've been doing this now, and I'm told that I've appeared on 39 local and national TV shows, promoting my books, including my self-published children's book. And also, interestingly enough, promoting my physical product. I told you I'm an entrepreneur. I have a gourmet food company, and I promote my products on local TV news shows all across the country. This makes me unlike any other gourmet food salesman in the marketplace. I call up a chef. I say, hey, chef, I'm here in Miami because I'm going to be on NBC Miami's Morning News tomorrow morning, and I was wondering if you want me to come by your kitchen to show you our products after I'm done at the studio. They always say yes. When I go on Facebook and Twitter and in social media and sending out to my email newsletter or news list, I send links to my video appearances promoting my books. No other independently self-published authors are promoting their books right now. Here's the interesting thing. These big publishers don't have the money to promote their book authors unless you're John Grisham, or the author of Fifty Shades of Grey. It's up to you to do it. The beautiful part is that because the big publishers aren't pushing their authors onto the shows, there's a huge vacuum. They need guests. They are hungry. They're happy for authors to call them up and pitch them a segment and promote their book on their new shows in the morning as long as you know what you're doing, as long as you know how to give them a segment that meets their needs as producers. Yes, ma'am. Okay, right. So the big idea, right, you can have the greatest product or service in the world, but if you're not getting the attention of your target market, you won't have a business. As technology increases, businesses are discovering it's getting tougher and tougher to grasp customers' attention. At last count, customers are bombarded with 3,191 marketing messages per day. They're overwhelmed, bored, switched off, and totally immune to the sea of traditional advertising. If your marketing blends in, you're throwing money down the drain. The key to business success in the new economy is standing out and getting your customers' attention. And that's where I come in. My name's Maria Neal, and in the next six minutes, you're going to hear about my 27 and one mobile marketing system and how it's changing the way businesses communicate with their customers and the new economy. Now, I've worked in various fields throughout my life, but whether it's been guiding others through treacherous rapids and a white water kayak, roping teams together to scale a mountain or driving trucks and hauling oversized loads across America and New Zealand, there's always been one common thread. My life and those of others have all come down to my ability and skill to not only spot dangers, hazards, and changes on the landscape, but take swift and decisive action to negotiate it safely. And now as a business owner, I can clearly see the changes on the horizon. Traditional forms of advertising and marketing are ineffective. Only 4% of emails are ever opened. Advertising salesmen continue to sell you space, not results. And you've spent thousands on websites and search optimization just to be told it all means nothing unless you own a Twitter account and a Facebook fan page. Businesses need real-time solutions to real-time problems. If your business is empty or slow today, designing, printing, delivering a new flyer takes time, money, and effort. And in the meantime, you're still struggling. Your problem is now, and that's why I give businesses the ability to reach through their customers' mobile, grab hold of them, and pull them directly through the doors of their business. No more downtime, slow days, or paying idle staff. With push-button simplicity, I help businesses boost sales and profits by harnessing the power of mobile, where they fully customize 27-in-1 mobile marketing system. Our system streamlines all your business information from various sources across the web and places it directly into the hands of your customers.
